text: The robotic surgical setup is visible as the surgeon prepares to address a medial recurrence of an inguinal hernia, likely following a previous mesh-based Lichtenstein repair.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap on the right side, dissecting laterally to medially through the umbilical ligaments and progressing towards the midline. The focus is on isolating the medial hernia without identifying the pubic tubercle.

The surgeon works laterally within the visceral compartment, developing the preperitoneal flap. The intermediate fascia is visible as the tissue is reflected up against the sidewall to minimize neuropathic pain.

The surgeon performs a limited lateral dissection, focusing on the intermediate fascia while working in the visceral compartment on both medial and lateral aspects. The dissection progresses towards the midline and approaches the pubis.
The surgeon identifies the incarcerated preperitoneal fat and locates the pseudosac. The hernia is then reduced, with the pseudosac clearly visible during the procedure.

The surgeon focuses on the retrocubic dissection, aggressively developing the space beyond two centimeters between Cooper's ligament and the bladder. The levator muscles and obturator internus are identified, with attention to the midline. The dissection extends beyond the midline to identify the contralateral Cooper's ligament for mesh fixation in the context of a medial hernia.

The surgeon continues to identify the contralateral Cooper's ligament, performing a medial dissection to develop the lateral plane. The spermatic vessels are reflected towards the side wall, and the vas deferens is parietalized. An adequate pocket is created, and the iliopsoas muscle is identified. A mid-weight mesh is prepared for placement in the pocket.

The surgeon examines the anatomy, identifying the lacunar ligament and the medial hernia. The indirect space is visualized, and the iliopubic tract is confirmed, ruling out a lipoma. The parietalization and retropubic dissection are assessed. A mid-weight mesh is prepared for placement, with plans to secure it at both the ipsilateral and contralateral Cooper's ligaments.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of leaving air knots unless bleeding occurs, to prevent mesh deformation. The peritoneal flap is closed from lateral to medial, starting with the lateral aspect under tension, followed by the redundant medial peritoneum.
The surgeon performs a standard repair, addressing a small fenestration in the preperitoneal space without evacuating air.
